prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a stanzaic catalogue of misfortunes — Weland bound by Niðhad's sinew-fetters, Beadohild's unwilling pregnancy, Mæðhild and Geat, Theodric's thirty winters in the Mærings' stronghold, the wolf-minded Eormanric ruling the Goths — each stanza closing with the same one-line refrain of consolation, before the speaker finally names himself as a scop displaced from his lord's favor by a rival named Heorrenda? Quote at least three occurrences of the refrain together with the speaker's self-naming, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The combination of (a) a stanzaic structure with an identical recurring refrain, (b) this exact catalogue of Germanic-legend exempla (Weland/Beadohild/Mæðhild/Theodric/Eormanric), and (c) a first-person scop dispossessed by Heorrenda is unique to this single chapter. Cynewulf's Andreas/Elene/Juliana are continuous Christian-saint narratives without refrains or pre-Christian legendary catalogues; no other corpus work uses Weland, Beadohild, or Heorrenda.

prompt: Which work in the corpus contemplates a shattered city as the 'work of giants', describing roofs toppled and a barred gate plundered, and then turns specifically to the city's many bath-houses — stone courts where 'a stream threw up heat in a wide surge', a wall enclosing all 'within its bright embrace' where the baths were 'hot at their heart', with hot streams poured 'over the grey stone' until 'the round pool grew hot'? Quote the bath passage and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: A meditation on a ruined city that pivots into a description of working hot-spring baths with a round heated pool inside enclosing walls is unique to this single chapter (a description widely identified as Roman Aquae Sulis). Cynewulf's Christian narratives contain no such ruin-meditation; the 'wind-blown walls' passage in The Wanderer (also in this corpus work) ends at the wall, with no baths, hot streams, or round pool. No other corpus work describes hypocaust-style heated baths.

prompt: Which work in the corpus contains a brief, riddle-like first-person lament in which the speaker says her people 'will devour him, should he dare to come in a raiding troop', insists 'our fates are not the same', describes her beloved as on one island while she is on another 'fast' island 'by fen surrounded', then suddenly turns to address a second man by name, telling him that 'our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods'? Quote the address to the named second man and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The trio of (a) two named figures — one called only as a wolf-named beloved across a fen-bound island, the other an addressee named Eadwacer — (b) the cryptic 'wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods' line, and (c) the refrain 'our fates are not the same' is unique to this single chapter. No Cynewulfian saint narrative has a riddling triangle of named lovers, and no other corpus work names Eadwacer or contains the whelp-borne-by-wolf image.
